title: Inline message vs toast notification vs error state vs inline validation
summary: Use an inline message for persistent contextual status beside one object or section; use toast for disposable transient feedback, error state for blocked recovery, and inline validation for one field's correction.
decisionRules: Choose inline message when the feedback belongs beside a visible row, panel, step, card, table section, or task area and users may need to read it while continuing nearby work.
Choose toast notification when the message is short, non-blocking, and safely disposable or recoverable from history after a few seconds.
Choose error state when expected content, saving, sync, payment, permission, or computation failed and the affected area needs persistent recovery controls.
Choose inline validation when the message is tied to one input value and tells users exactly how to correct that field.
Use inline message for contextual warnings such as selected customers missing billing contacts, export limitations, draft conflict notes, or local success that should stay near the affected object.
Do not use inline message as a detached page banner; if the message affects the whole page, service, or site, use the appropriate page-level or global feedback surface.
Do not place a large troubleshooting workflow inside an inline message; include one local action or disclosure and route deeper repair to an error state, panel, page, or dialog.
Announce dynamic inline messages as status or alert according to urgency without moving focus unless the user requested the message or must act immediately.
failureModes: A warning about one table row appears at the top of the page, so users cannot tell which item is affected.
A field-format correction is shown as a section message instead of being attached to the input.
A blocking load failure is reduced to a small inline note without retry or fallback.
A success toast disappears even though users need to copy the generated reference beside the object.
A local message contains several unrelated links, long support instructions, and multi-step form fields.
A dynamic inline warning appears visually but is not announced to assistive technology.

sources.note: Atlassian defines inline message as a way to indicate important information or action availability in context.
Carbon distinguishes inline notifications from toast and modal variants and places inline notifications in task flows near related content.
React Spectrum describes inline alerts as non-modal messages associated with objects in a view.
WCAG status-message guidance supports announcing dynamically added messages without taking focus.
